1: ho echōn ta hepta pneumata tou theou kai tous hepta asteras: 'the one who holds the seven Spirits of God and the seven stars.' The self-identification shifts from the description in Revelation 1 — here the seven Spirits of God (the fullness of the Spirit) are in Christ's possession, linking his authority directly to the Spirit's discernment. Oida sou ta erga, hoti onoma echeis hoti zēis, kai nekros ei: I know your works — you have a reputation for being alive, but you are dead. The contrast is devastating: external reputation (onoma, name/reputation) versus actual spiritual state.
2: ginou grēgorōn kai stērison ta loipa ha emellon apothanein: 'Wake up, and strengthen what remains that is about to die.' Ginou grēgorōn: become watchful, a continuous state. Ta loipa (the rest, the remnant): what survives of the community's original vitality. Ou gar heurēka sou ta erga peplērōmena enōpion tou theou mou: I have not found your works complete/full before my God — peplērōmena (perfect passive participle): the works were started but not completed, and the incomplete state persists.
3: ean oun mē grēgorēsēs, hēxō hōs kleptēs, kai ou mē gnōs poian hōran hēxō epi se: 'If you will not watch, I will come like a thief, and you will not know at what hour I will come against you.' The thief-coming (hōs kleptēs) is explicitly an unexpected judgment-coming, not the final parousia — Christ comes in historical judgment on unfaithful churches within time. The same hōs kleptēs metaphor appears in Matthew 24:43; 1 Thessalonians 5:2; 2 Peter 3:10 for the parousia, but here it is specifically the imminent intra-historical judgment.
4: alla echeis oliga onomata en Sardesi ha ouk emolynon ta himatia autōn: 'Yet you have a few names in Sardis who have not soiled their garments.' Onomata (names): individuals; the few faithfully remaining are described by the Hebrew idiom of naming — they are known to God by name. Ouk emolynon ta himatia (did not defile their garments): the metaphor of soiled clothing for moral/spiritual compromise is common in Second Temple literature. Peripathsousin met emou en leukois: they will walk with me in white — the clean garments of the faithful are the eschatological reward.
5: ho nikōn houtōs peribaleitai en himatios leukois, kai ou mē exaleipsō to onoma autou ek tēs biblou tēs zōēs: 'The one who overcomes will be clothed in white garments, and I will never blot out his name from the book of life.' The double negative ou mē exaleipsō (I will absolutely never blot out): the strongest negative construction in Greek, providing the most emphatic assurance possible. Exaleipsō (blot out) recalls Moses's intercession (Exodus 32:32-33) and Daniel 12:1; the promise inverts the threat of erasure with the certainty of permanent inscription.
6: ho echōn ous akousatō ti to pneuma legei tais ekklēsiais: Standard closing formula. The repeated formula across all seven letters creates a liturgical rhythm — each letter ends identically, reinforcing that the Spirit's address to one community is for all.
7: ho hagios, ho alēthinos, ho echōn tēn kleida Dauid, ho anoigōn kai oudeis kleisei, kai kleiōn kai oudeis anoigei: 'the holy one, the true one, who has the key of David, who opens and no one shuts, who shuts and no one opens.' Hagios and alēthinos (holy and true) are divine attributes applied directly to Christ. The key-of-David language quotes Isaiah 22:22 (the investiture of Eliakim as royal steward). The two present participles anoigōn and kleiōn in an antithetical pair with oudeis (no one) twice: absolute, unqualified authority over access to the divine household.
8: idou dedōka enōpion sou thyran ēnoigmenēn, hēn oudeis dynatai kleisai autēn: 'Behold, I have placed before you an open door that no one is able to shut.' Dedōka (perfect): I have given and it stands given — the open door is an accomplished and ongoing reality. Ēnoigmenēn (perfect passive participle): the door has been opened and remains open. Dynamin mikran (little power/strength): Philadelphia is weak by worldly measures; the open door is given to the weak church, not the strong one.
9: idou didō ek tēs synagōgēs tou satana tōn legontōn heautous Ioudaious einai kai ouk eisin alla pseudontai: 'Behold, I will make those of the synagogue of Satan who say they are Jews and are not, but lie.' The future poiēsō autous hina hēxousin kai proskyneousin enōpion tōn podōn sou (I will make them come and bow down at your feet): this is an inversion of the Isaianic promises where nations bow before restored Israel (Isaiah 49:23; Revelation 60:14); the persecutors will acknowledge the vindication of the oppressed community. Kagō ēgapēsa se: that I have loved you (aorist) — the vindication proves the prior love.
10: hoti etērēsas ton logon tēs hypomonēs mou, kagō se tērēsō ek tēs hōras tou peirasmou: 'Because you have kept the word of my patient endurance, I will also keep you from the hour of trial.' The symmetry of keeping: the community kept Christ's word; Christ keeps the community. Tēs horas tou peirasmou (the hour of trial): possibly a specific future tribulation period or more generally the eschatological pressure. Ek tēs hōras (from the hour): the preposition ek (out from) is debated — does it mean preservation through the trial or removal before it begins?
11: erchomai tachy; kratei ho echeis, hina mēdeis labē ton stephanon sou: 'I am coming quickly. Hold fast what you have, so that no one may seize your crown.' Erchomai tachy: present-tense coming, imminent arrival. Kratei (aorist imperative): seize/hold firm — decisive act of retention. Ton stephanon sou: your crown — the stephanos (victor's wreath) can be seized by another if you do not persevere; crowns in Revelation are conditionally held.
12: ho nikōn, poiēsō auton stylon en tō naō tou theou mou: 'The one who overcomes, I will make him a pillar in the temple of my God.' Stylon (pillar): structural element, immovable, permanent — contrasts with Philadelphia's earthquake vulnerability (major earthquakes struck the region ca. 17 CE; pillars that stood were the prize of survivors). Exō ouk exeleusetai eti: he will go out no more — the conqueror's place in the divine temple is permanent, unlike a city that might be evacuated for safety. The threefold writing on the pillar (name of God, name of the city, Christ's new name) inscribes triple identity on the victor.
14: ho Amēn, ho martys ho pistos kai alēthinos, hē archē tēs ktiseōs tou theou: 'the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the beginning of God's creation.' Ho Amēn as a title: drawn from Isaiah 65:16 (elohei amen, God of the Amen/God of truth). Archē tēs ktiseōs (the beginning of creation): this is the most debated phrase in the seven letters. Archē can mean first-in-time (as Arians argued: the first created being) or source/ruler (as orthodox interpreters argued, citing Colossians 1:15-18's firstborn-of-creation language meaning sovereign over creation). The context — presenting Christ as the one who holds the Seven Spirits and seven stars, who is divine witness and divine Amen — supports the ruler/source reading.
15: oida sou ta erga, hoti oute psychros ei oute zestos; ophelon psychros ēs ē zestos: 'I know your works: you are neither cold nor hot; would that you were cold or hot.' The commercial metaphor uses temperature to describe spiritual vitality. Cold water (from Colossae) and hot water (from Hierapolis) both had uses; Laodicea's lukewarm water supply (from mineral springs) was medically unpleasant. The lukewarm church has no distinctiveness — neither refreshing nor curative, merely tepid.
16: houtōs, hoti chlioros ei kai oute zestos oute psychros, mellō se emesai ek tou stomatos mou: 'So, because you are lukewarm, and neither hot nor cold, I will spit you out of my mouth.' Emesai (vomit out): strong visceral imagery — the reaction of a body to something that causes nausea. Mellō (I am about to): the expulsion is imminent, not yet enacted — the conditional structure of Revelation 3:19-20 implies repentance can avert it. The severity of the language to the wealthiest of the seven churches is deliberate.
17: hoti legeis hoti plousios eimi kai peploutēka kai ouden chreian echō: 'Because you say, I am rich and I have prospered and I need nothing.' Plousios (rich) and peploutēka (I have grown rich, perfect) — Laodicea was one of the wealthiest cities in Asia, known for banking, textile manufacture, and medicine. The perfect tense peploutēka expresses the settled self-confidence of accumulated wealth. The five-fold description of their actual state — wretched, pitiable, poor, blind, naked — inverts the five-fold prosperity they claim.
18: symbouleuō soi agorasai par emou chrysion pepyrōmenon ek pyros hina ploutēsēs: 'I counsel you to buy from me gold refined by fire, so that you may be rich.' The commercial metaphor uses marketplace language against Laodicea's commercial pride — they are to buy from Christ. Chrysion pepyrōmenon ek pyros (gold refined/purified by fire): true spiritual wealth earned through testing. Kollyrion (eye salve): Laodicea was famous for Phrygian powder used as a medical eye treatment; the city's pharmacological reputation becomes the ironic vehicle for Christ's offer of genuine spiritual sight.
19: egō hosous ean philō elegchō kai paideuō; zēleue oun kai metanoēson: 'Those whom I love, I rebuke and discipline. So be zealous and repent.' Elegchō (rebuke/convict) and paideuō (discipline/train): both terms carry the flavor of parental correction (Proverbs 3:12; Hebrews 12:6). The severity of the Laodicea letter is thus reframed as love's most direct form. Zēleue (aorist imperative): be zealous, be fired up — the antidote to lukewarmness is not moderation but ignition.
20: idou hestēka epi tēn thyran kai krouō; ean tis akousē tēs phōnēs mou kai anoixē tēn thyran, eiseleusomai pros auton kai deipnēsō met autou kai autos met emou: 'Behold, I have been standing at the door and knocking; if anyone hears my voice and opens the door, I will come in and eat with him, and he with me.' Hestēka (perfect): I have been standing and stand — ongoing presence, not a momentary visit. Krouō (present): continuous knocking. The deipnon (evening meal, the main meal of the day) implies covenant fellowship — shared table in antiquity was the most intimate expression of relationship and commitment. Notably this promise is to individuals within the church (tis, anyone), not the community as a whole; Laodicea is so far gone that Christ knocks for any individual willing to open.
21: ho nikōn, dōsō autō kathisai met emou en tō thronō mou, hōs kagō enikekon kai ekathisa meta tou patros mou en tō thronō autou: 'The one who overcomes, I will grant him to sit with me on my throne, as I also overcame and sat down with my Father on his throne.' The highest promise in the seven letters: sharing Christ's throne. Enikekon (perfect): I have conquered and remain in the state of having conquered — the perfect here captures the ongoing ruling status of the risen Christ at the Father's right hand (Psalms 110:1; Hebrews 1:3). The conqueror participates in what Christ has already accomplished: the promise extends Christ's own victory-throne to the faithful community.
